title: Generics 🧬
description: Writing code once for multiple data types.

slides.title: The Duplication Problem 👯
slides.content:
Imagine you write a function to find the largest `i32` in a list. Great!

Now you need to find the largest `f64`. Do you copy-paste the exact same function and just change the type signature? No! That's bad practice.

slides.title: Generics to the Rescue 🧬
slides.content:
Generics allow you to write a function that takes a placeholder type, usually written as `<T>`. 

This means 'This function works for some Type T, I'll tell you what T is later when I actually call the function!'

slides.title: A Generic Function 🧩
slides.content: Notice how we declare `<T>` right after the function name.

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of using Generics (`<T>`) in Rust?

slides.title: Zero Cost Abstraction 🚀
slides.content:
You might think generics slow down your program because it has to figure out the type at runtime. 

WRONG! Rust uses **Monomorphization**. At compile time, Rust secretly generates a unique, lightning-fast copy of your function for *every single type* you called it with. It runs exactly as fast as if you hand-wrote separate functions!
